pipeline.name: huu Performance Audit
pipeline.description: Static hotspot scan (N+1, big-O, sync I/O, memory leaks, unbounded concurrency, missing caching), a Core Web Vitals scorecard and a USE-method checklist. Report-only.

steps.name: 1. Classify project, install profilers, scaffold .huu/audits/performance.md
steps.prompt:
You are huu's performance-audit bootstrap agent. Goal: classify the project (frontend / backend / library / CLI / mobile), make ephemeral profilers available, write `.huu/audits/performance.md` scaffold, initialize `.huu/audits/performance-faq.json`.

=== STEP 0 — REPORT-ONLY HARD RULE ===
You may NOT modify any file in the repo OTHER than the audit artifacts under `.huu/audits/` (and `.huu/audits/.tmp/` for working files) plus the single `.gitignore` adjustment described below. If a tool requires installation, use `npx --yes <tool>` (Node), `pipx run <tool>` (Python), or vendored binaries under `$HOME/.huu/bin/`. NEVER touch package.json, requirements.txt, pyproject.toml, Cargo.toml, go.mod, lockfiles, or any production source. Create the output directory with `mkdir -p .huu/audits/.tmp/performance` before writing.

=== PERSISTENCE CHECK ===
The deliverables under `.huu/audits/` must survive the stage merge, so they cannot be gitignored.
Run: `git check-ignore -q .huu/audits && echo IGNORED || echo OK`
If IGNORED: the committed `.gitignore` excludes `.huu/`. Apply the MINIMAL rewrite — replace the line `.huu/` (or `.huu`) with `.huu/*` and add `!.huu/audits/` on the next line. Git cannot re-include below an excluded DIRECTORY, but `.huu/*` only excludes the entries, so the negation works. This one-line edit is the ONLY permitted change outside `.huu/audits/` in this pipeline. If OK: leave `.gitignore` alone.

=== STEP 1 — Classify the project ===
Inspect the manifests and pick the BEST single classification (or "mixed"):
- **frontend**: package.json has react/vue/svelte/next/nuxt/angular, plus an entry like `vite`/`webpack`/`next` config. Public assets folder.
- **backend**: package.json has express/fastify/koa/hapi/nestjs OR pyproject.toml has flask/fastapi/django OR go.mod with web framework OR Java with Spring etc.
- **cli**: package.json bin field, or a Python entrypoint, or Go/Rust binary target without web routes.
- **library**: exports a public API, no bin/entry server, package.json has `main`/`exports`/`types`.
- **mobile**: react-native, Flutter (pubspec.yaml), Android (build.gradle), iOS (.xcodeproj).
- **mixed**: doesn't fit one clearly (e.g. monorepo).

Note the classification — subsequent steps key off it.

=== STEP 2 — Make profilers available (ephemeral only) ===
EPHEMERAL ONLY. Use one-shot runners that DON'T modify the repo's manifests:

**frontend**
- `npx --yes @lhci/cli@latest collect <args>` (Lighthouse CI for Core Web Vitals).
- `npx --yes source-map-explorer <args>` for bundle weight.
- `npx --yes vite-bundle-visualizer <args>` for Vite projects.

**backend (Node)**
- `npx --yes clinic <args>` (clinic.js).
- `npx --yes autocannon <url>` (load gen — DO NOT add to devDeps).

**backend (Python)**
- `pipx run py-spy <args>`.
- `pipx run scalene <args>`.

**Go**
- Install pprof to ephemeral bin: `GOBIN=$HOME/.huu/bin go install github.com/google/pprof@latest`. NEVER add to the project.

**Rust**
- Use `cargo clippy` if already configured. Skip flamegraph if not (heavy install).

If a tool is not reachable: append an info finding and continue. The pipeline NEVER hard-fails on missing tools.

=== STEP 3 — Write .huu/audits/performance.md scaffold ===
Path: `./.huu/audits/performance.md`. Required structure:

# .huu/audits/performance.md — Performance audit

> Report-only static performance audit generated by huu's Performance Audit pipeline.

## 1. Classification
- Project type: <frontend|backend|cli|library|mobile|mixed>
- Languages: <list>
- Available profilers: <list>
- Unavailable profilers: <list with reason>

## 2. Static hotspot scan
(filled in by the per-file hotspot scan)

## 3. Frontend metrics (Core Web Vitals)
(filled in by step 3 — only if classification = frontend or mixed)

## 4. USE checklist (Brendan Gregg)
(filled in by step 4 — only if classification = backend / cli / mixed)

## 5. Bundle / artifact weight
(filled in by step 4 — frontend & library)

## 6. Recommendations
(filled in by step 5)

## 7. Validation
(filled in by the final step after the judge approves)

=== STEP 4 — Initialize .huu/audits/performance-faq.json ===
`./.huu/audits/performance-faq.json`. Standard idempotency rules. Schema:
```json
{ "summary": "<=256>", "knowledge": "<=5000>", "path": "<file or 'global'>", "category": "n+1|big-o|sync-io|memory|render-blocking|use-method|cwv|bundle", "severity": "info|warn|critical", "metric": { "name": "...", "value": ... } }
```
Optional per-entry prioritization fields (additive — entries without them remain valid): "priority": 1|2|3 (1 = do first), "fixability": "trivial|moderate|involved".

=== HARD RULES ===
- DO NOT modify source code.
- DO NOT run benchmarks against external services or production.
- Tool installs only — no other changes outside the new artifacts.

steps.name: 2. Select hotspot-scan targets
steps.prompt:
You are huu's performance-hotspot target selector. Goal: choose the files worth the work that follows and hand them to the parallel fan-out — ONE cognitive op: pick the files, write the list, nothing else.

=== SKIP RULE (never list these) ===
Generated/vendored/trivial paths: `node_modules/`, `dist/`, `build/`, `out/`, `coverage/`, `.git/`, `vendor/`, `target/`, `__pycache__/`, `*.generated.*`, `*.min.js`, `*.min.css`, `*.d.ts`, `*.lock`, `*.snap`; and pure config / constants / type-only / barrel-export files.

=== SELECT (at most 30, most valuable first) ===
A file earns a slot only when scanning for static performance hotspots in it would catch real regressions or surface real findings. Prefer:
- hot paths — request/event handlers, loops over collections, DB/ORM access, data transforms, serialization
- files doing synchronous I/O, heavy computation, or unbounded concurrency
- render-critical frontend components and anything on the startup / request critical path
Fewer, load-bearing files beat a long shallow list. Rank by value and let the cap drop the tail.

=== WRITE THE MEMORY FILE ===
Write your selection to the file named in the MEMORY CONTRACT appended below (huu fills in the exact path + format). One entry per file:
- `path`: repo-relative.
- `hint`: name the suspected hotspot (N+1 query, nested loop / big-O, sync I/O, memory leak, render-blocking) — this becomes the next agent's `$hint` and is the single most valuable thing you hand over. Never leave it empty.
- `priority`: higher runs first; the cap drops the lowest. Rank deliberately.
An EMPTY list is valid and means "nothing here qualifies" — the next stage then runs zero tasks (do not pad to hit the cap).

=== HARD RULES ===
- Selection only: do NOT modify source, run long builds, or write anything except the memory file.
- Be language-agnostic — do not assume Node.js.
- Every path you list must exist in the working tree (the consumer drops unresolvable paths).

=== BEFORE YOU START ===
Read `./.huu/audits/performance.md` section 1 (the scaffold step recorded the project classification — frontend / backend / library / CLI / mobile / mixed). Lean toward the files that classification makes risky (frontend → render-critical components; backend → request handlers and DB access). This step writes ONLY the target list; the per-file hotspot scan that follows does the actual finding.

steps.name: 3. Static hotspot scan: $file
steps.prompt:
You are the static hotspot scan for ONE file: `$file`. You are one of many agents running in parallel; your whole job is this single file. Goal: identify likely performance hotspots in this single file and append findings to `.huu/audits/performance-faq.json`. NO code changes.

The recon step chose this file deliberately and left you a lead — start there: $hint

=== STEP 0 — SKIP RULE ===
SKIP IMMEDIATELY (no findings, no FAQ append) if `$file` matches: `node_modules/`, `dist/`, `build/`, `out/`, `coverage/`, `.git/`, `vendor/`, `target/`, `__pycache__/`, `*.generated.*`, `*.min.js`, `*.min.css`, `*.d.ts`, `*.lock`, `*.snap`.

=== STEP 1 — Read inputs ===
- `./.huu/audits/performance.md` (note the classification in section 1).
- `./.huu/audits/performance-faq.json` (may be empty).
- `$file`.

=== STEP 2 — Patterns to detect ===

**N+1 query patterns**
- Loop over collection, fetch related record inside loop:
  - JS/TS: `for (const x of xs) { await fetch(`/api/${x.id}`) }`, `xs.forEach(async x => { await db.find(...) })`, ORM patterns: `users.forEach(u => u.posts)` without eager load.
  - Python (Django/SQLAlchemy): `for u in users: u.posts` without `prefetch_related`/`selectinload`.
  - Ruby (ActiveRecord): `users.each { |u| u.posts }` without `includes`.
- Severity: warn unless inside a public HTTP handler / endpoint route → critical.

**O(n²)+ hotspots**
- Nested loops over user-controlled input (request body, query params, file contents).
- `.find()` / `.indexOf()` inside `.map()` / `.filter()` / `.forEach()` on the same collection.
- Severity: warn unless input size is unbounded → critical.

**Sync I/O in hot paths**
- `readFileSync`, `writeFileSync`, `execSync`, `fs.statSync` inside an HTTP handler / event loop tick.
- Blocking sleep in async code (`await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, ms))` is OK; `spinLoop` is not).
- Python: synchronous `requests.get` inside an `async def` (a common bug).
- Severity: warn for utility code; critical for request-handling code.

**Memory leak signals**
- `addEventListener` without matching `removeEventListener` (also `.on()` without `.off()`).
- Global / module-level mutable collections that only grow.
- `setInterval` without `clearInterval` referenced in the same scope.
- Severity: warn.

**Render-blocking / bundle-bloat (frontend only)**
- Large `import` of an entire library where tree-shake is available (`import * as lodash from 'lodash'` vs `import debounce from 'lodash/debounce'`).
- Synchronous `<script>` tags in HTML head.
- `require()` calls inside React component bodies (forces re-evaluation on render).
- Severity: warn.

**Inefficient regex**
- `(.+)+`, `(a*)*`, catastrophic-backtracking shapes.
- Regex compilation inside a loop (move to module scope).
- Severity: warn unless on user input → critical.

**Unbounded concurrency** (Azure antipatterns: improper instantiation)
- `Promise.all(items.map(...))` over an unbounded/user-controlled list (no p-limit/chunking).
- A new client per request: `new S3Client()` / `new PrismaClient()` / `http.Agent` constructed inside a handler instead of module scope.
- Severity: warn; critical when the list size is user-controlled.

**Missing caching / extraneous fetching** (Azure antipatterns: no-caching, extraneous fetching)
- The same remote/DB fetch repeated with identical arguments inside one request lifecycle.
- `SELECT *` / full-collection fetches followed by in-memory filtering of a handful of fields; endpoints returning unbounded lists with no pagination.
- Severity: warn.

=== STEP 3 — Append findings ===
For EACH match found:
```json
{ "summary": "$file: <line>: <pattern> (<short context>)", "knowledge": "<the offending code snippet, the rule it triggered, suggested fix in 1-2 sentences>", "path": "$file", "category": "n+1|big-o|sync-io|memory|render-blocking", "severity": "info|warn|critical" }
```

If `$file` has zero findings, append ONE info-severity confirmation.

=== HARD RULES ===
- DO NOT modify `$file` or any other file.
- DO NOT run tests / benchmarks (this step is static analysis only).
- Append-only to the FAQ (re-read before each append; parallel agents).

steps.name: 4. Frontend Core Web Vitals
steps.prompt: You are at step 3 — frontend Core Web Vitals. Goal: if the project classifies as a frontend, attempt to capture LCP / INP / CLS via lighthouse-ci (or a static heuristic if not). Append findings, populate section "3. Frontend metrics" of `.huu/audits/performance.md`. SKIP this step gracefully for non-frontend projects. === PROGRESSIVE KNOWLEDGE PROTOCOL === `./.huu/audits/performance-faq.json` is this run's shared memory — a single JSON array every agent reads and extends. 1. BEFORE acting: read `./.huu/audits/performance-faq.json`. Build on what prior steps/agents already discovered; do not re-derive solved facts or re-flag findings that are already recorded. 2. AFTER your goal: RE-READ `./.huu/audits/performance-faq.json` (parallel agents may have appended while you worked), then APPEND your entries: { "summary": "<=256>", "knowledge": "<=5000>", "path": "", "category": "n+1|big-o|sync-io|memory|render-blocking|use-method|cwv|bundle", "severity": "info|warn|critical", "metric": { "name": "...", "value": ... } } Optionally add prioritization fields when you can judge them: "priority": 1|2|3 (1 = do first) and "fixability": "trivial|moderate|involved" — the final step orders its recommendations by them. 3. Append-only: never rewrite, reorder, or delete existing entries. Skip the append when a semantically equivalent summary already exists. 4. If `./.huu/audits/performance-faq.json` is missing: abort with a clear error (the bootstrap step is a prerequisite). If it is corrupted: replace it with `[]` and say so in the log. In particular: step 2's per-file findings under "render-blocking" and "bundle" point at the files most likely to explain a bad LCP — cross-reference them in your "knowledge" field instead of re-discovering them. === STEP 1 — Check classification === Read `./.huu/audits/performance.md` section 1. If classification is NOT frontend or mixed: write "N/A — non-frontend project" into section "3. Frontend metrics" and finish. === STEP 2 — Build the app (best-effort) === Try common build commands in order: - `npm run build` - `yarn build` - `pnpm build` - `vite build` If none works, skip to step 4 (static fallback). === STEP 3 — Run Lighthouse against the built bundle === If `@lhci/cli` was installed in step 1: - Identify the dev/preview server command (`npm run preview`, `npx serve dist`, etc.). - Start it in the background on a free port. - Run: `npx --yes lighthouse "http://localhost:" --output=json --output-path=./.huu/audits/.tmp/performance/lighthouse.json --chrome-flags="--headless --no-sandbox --disable-gpu"`. - Parse the JSON to extract `audits.largest-contentful-paint.numericValue`, `audits.total-blocking-time.numericValue`, `audits.cumulative-layout-shift.numericValue`, `audits.first-contentful-paint.numericValue`. - Stop the background server. Targets (Google's published thresholds, assessed at the 75th percentile in the field, https://web.dev/articles/vitals): - LCP: good <= 2500 ms, poor > 4000 ms. - INP: good <= 200 ms, poor > 500 ms. **Lab caveat (web.dev): Lighthouse cannot measure INP (no real user input) — report Total Blocking Time (TBT) as the lab PROXY for INP (good <= 200 ms, poor > 600 ms) and say so explicitly in the scorecard. A real INP number requires field/RUM data (CrUX).** - CLS: good <= 0.1, poor > 0.25. - FCP: good <= 1800 ms, poor > 3000 ms. === STEP 4 — Static fallback (if Lighthouse couldn't run) === Identify potential bottlenecks WITHOUT running the app: - Total JS bundle size from `dist/` / `build/` / `.next/` (warn > 200 KB gzipped, critical > 500 KB). - Inline `

steps.name: 5. USE checklist + bundle weight
steps.prompt:
You are at step 4 — USE checklist (backend / CLI) and bundle weight (frontend / library). Skip the irrelevant half based on classification.

=== PROGRESSIVE KNOWLEDGE PROTOCOL ===
`./.huu/audits/performance-faq.json` is this run's shared memory — a single JSON array every agent reads and extends.
1. BEFORE acting: read `./.huu/audits/performance-faq.json`. Build on what prior steps/agents already discovered; do not re-derive solved facts or re-flag findings that are already recorded.
2. AFTER your goal: RE-READ `./.huu/audits/performance-faq.json` (parallel agents may have appended while you worked), then APPEND your entries:
   { "summary": "<=256>", "knowledge": "<=5000>", "path": "<file or 'global'>", "category": "n+1|big-o|sync-io|memory|render-blocking|use-method|cwv|bundle", "severity": "info|warn|critical", "metric": { "name": "...", "value": ... } }
   Optionally add prioritization fields when you can judge them: "priority": 1|2|3 (1 = do first) and "fixability": "trivial|moderate|involved" — the final step orders its recommendations by them.
3. Append-only: never rewrite, reorder, or delete existing entries. Skip the append when a semantically equivalent summary already exists.
4. If `./.huu/audits/performance-faq.json` is missing: abort with a clear error (the bootstrap step is a prerequisite). If it is corrupted: replace it with `[]` and say so in the log.
In particular: step 2's "sync-io" and "n+1" findings tell you which software resources (DB pool, event loop, worker queue) deserve a USE row — start the checklist from them.

=== STEP 1 — Branch on classification ===
Read `./.huu/audits/performance.md` section 1.
- If backend / cli / mixed → fill USE method checklist (section "4").
- If frontend / library / mixed → measure bundle/artifact weight (section "5").

Both can run for mixed projects.

=== STEP 2A — USE method checklist (Brendan Gregg, https://www.brendangregg.com/usemethod.html) ===
For each system resource the project actively uses, document the THREE columns: Utilization, Saturation, Errors. You are doing a STATIC pre-flight — actual measurement needs the running app. The goal is to identify WHICH metrics matter for this project and list the canonical commands to check them once it's running.

| Resource | Utilization | Saturation | Errors |
|---|---|---|---|
| CPU | top, mpstat -P ALL, htop | runqueue (vmstat r), load avg | mpstat -P (some platforms report ECC) |
| Memory | free, vmstat | si/so paging (vmstat), swap usage | dmesg \| grep -i oom |
| Disk I/O | iostat -xz 1 | iostat avgqu-sz / aqu-sz, %util > 80% | dmesg \| grep -i error |
| Network | sar -n DEV 1, ifconfig | netstat -s (overruns, drops), TCP retransmits | netstat -i (errors), `ss -i` |
| File descriptors | `lsof -p <pid> \| wc -l` | rlimit close to `ulimit -n` | EMFILE in logs |
| Open connections | `ss -tan` | conn-tracker table size | TCP refused/reset metrics |

For SOFTWARE resources (project-specific):
- DB connection pool: usage vs max; "wait for connection" events.
- Worker thread pool / async queue: depth; rejected enqueues.
- Cache: hit rate; eviction rate; missing-on-write conflicts.

=== STEP 2B — Bundle / artifact weight (frontend / library) ===
- Frontend: total bundle size from `dist/` / `build/` / `.next/` / `out/`. Gzipped if possible.
- Library: published artifact size (`npm pack --dry-run --json`). Warn if > 100 KB unpacked.
- Compare against industry rules:
  - Frontend critical-path JS gzip: > 200 KB = warn, > 500 KB = critical.
  - Library unpackedSize: > 1 MB = warn, > 5 MB = critical.

=== STEP 3 — Append findings and update sections "4" and "5" ===
For each resource where a likely concern is identified (e.g. project uses a DB but has no connection-pool config), append a USE-method finding:
```json
{ "summary": "DB pool size hardcoded to 10 — saturation risk under > 50 req/s", "knowledge": "<details, including the file + line>", "path": "<file>", "category": "use-method", "severity": "warn" }
```

For bundle weight:
```json
{ "summary": "dist/main.js = 720 KB gzip — exceeds 500 KB critical threshold", "knowledge": "<top-5 contributing modules from source-map-explorer if available, else 'install source-map-explorer for module-level breakdown'>", "path": "global", "category": "bundle", "severity": "critical", "metric": { "name": "bundle_gzip_kb", "value": 720 } }
```

=== HARD RULES ===
- DO NOT modify source.
- DO NOT delete the bundle artifacts (the user may want to inspect them).

steps.name: 6. Composite score + recommendations
steps.prompt:
You are the final agent — step 5. Goal: read all findings from `.huu/audits/performance-faq.json`, prioritize them, populate section "6. Recommendations" of `.huu/audits/performance.md`. No production-file edits.

=== STEP 1 — Read inputs ===
- `./.huu/audits/performance.md` (sections 1–5 already populated).
- `./.huu/audits/performance-faq.json`.

=== STEP 2 — Compute composite perf score ===
Start at 100. Subtract per finding:
- critical: -8 (cap -50).
- warn: -2 (cap -30).
- info: 0.

The counts you cite MUST match the FAQ entries exactly — the validation judge recounts them.

=== STEP 3 — Populate section "6. Recommendations" ===
Group by category, prioritize by severity.
Within each severity group, order items by "priority" (1 first) then "fixability" (trivial first) when the findings carry those fields; findings without them keep their relative order.

### Critical hotspots
- Cite each by `<file>:<line>` and give a one-liner fix.

### Warnings
- Same format.

### Frontend-specific (if applicable)
- "LCP is 3.2s — investigate render-blocking JS in <head>."
- "Bundle is 720 KB gzip — code-split with React.lazy / dynamic import()."

### Backend / CLI-specific (if applicable)
- "Wire pool metrics → Grafana before going to prod."
- "Add an integration test that exercises the USE method's saturation row for DB connections."

=== HARD RULES ===
- DO NOT modify any production file. The only output is `.huu/audits/performance.md`.
- If you computed a composite score, mention it as a markdown line in the report itself. Do NOT add a README badge.
- DO NOT skip the report even if there are no critical findings — write "No critical findings — keep the audit alive in CI" recommendation.

steps.name: 7. Validate report
steps.condition: The report at `.huu/audits/performance.md` is complete and internally consistent. Verify ALL of: 1) the file exists and every required section ("1. Classification", "2. Static hotspot scan", "3. Frontend metrics (Core Web Vitals)", "4. USE checklist (Brendan Gregg)", "5. Bundle / artifact weight", "6. Recommendations") is present and contains real content — no "(filled in by step N)" or TODO placeholders left. 2) `.huu/audits/performance-faq.json` parses as a JSON array and the counts cited in the report's summary tables match the actual entries (severity totals, category totals). 3) recommendations are ordered critical → warn → info, and within each severity respect the optional "priority"/"fixability" fields when present. 4) `git status --porcelain` shows NO modified files outside `.huu/` except at most one `.gitignore` whose diff only rewrites a `.huu/` exclusion into `.huu/*` + a negation line (report-only contract). 5) sections 3/4/5 explicitly say "N/A — <reason>" when the classification makes them inapplicable (a placeholder left blank is a failure, an explicit N/A is not), and any lab-only Core Web Vitals scorecard labels INP as the TBT proxy. This is run $runs of this validation. If every clause holds, answer "approved"; if any fails, answer "rework" and say precisely which clause failed and why in the reason.

steps.name: 8. Finalize report
steps.prompt:
You are the final agent — step 7 (post-validation). The judge approved the report. Goal: stamp section "7. Validation" of `.huu/audits/performance.md` and leave the working tree clean.

=== STEP 1 — Stamp the validation section ===
Replace the "## 7. Validation" placeholder with:
```
Validated: <UTC timestamp> · findings: <total> (critical <X> / warn <Y> / info <Z>) · composite score <N>/100 · sections 1–6 complete.
Lab metrics caveat preserved: INP reported via its lab proxy (TBT) when only Lighthouse data was available.
Validation gate: judge CheckStep ("approved" required to reach this step; "rework" loops back to consolidation, max 2 judge runs).
```
Numbers MUST come from re-reading `.huu/audits/performance-faq.json` and the report — do not invent them. Be idempotent: if the section is already stamped (a rework loop ran twice), overwrite it with fresh numbers.

=== STEP 2 — Exit hygiene ===
- Delete any leftovers under `./.huu/audits/.tmp/performance/` (keep the directory).
- Confirm `git status --porcelain` shows changes only under `.huu/` (plus at most the permitted `.gitignore` adjustment).

=== HARD RULES ===
- DO NOT modify the findings or the recommendations — stamp only.
- DO NOT touch production files.
